Throughout his interview, Isaac worked
hard to portray himself as a victim,
bringing up his past, the friends he's
lost, and how deeply this event has
traumatized him. He likely believes he's
convincing that he's playing the role
well. But what Isaac doesn't realize is
how quickly things are about to turn
against him.
By the time officers come into Kiara's
interrogation room to begin the
interview, she's already been left alone
for more than 3 hours. Plenty of time to
think. In that silence, she began
forming a plan, not to deny the murder
outright, but to find a way to minimize
her responsibility. Kiara knows there's
no escaping the fact that a murder took
place. [music] The body has been found
and all evidence points directly to her.
But she's not planning to walk out
innocent. She's planning to walk out
with a story.
If she can act as though she's mentally
unstable, there's a chance she'll be
deemed not guilty by reason of insanity.
This, in her eyes, is a much less severe
outcome, which will get her back to the
real world in no time. This is a common
misconception. Legal insanity isn't
about acting strange after arrest. It's
about your mental state at the moment of
the crime. Detectives record everything.
And criminal psychologists are trained
to spot rehearsed behavior and
malingering. Even if a judge believes
you are genuinely insane, you don't go
home. You go to a locked forensic
hospital with no fixed release date. If
you aren't genuinely crippled by mental
illness, the constant observation,
enforced medication, and clinical
evaluations become their own punishment.
Kiara doesn't realize this, so she'll
begin the interrogation with confidence
that she can outsmart the system.
